title: Introduction to inheritance

exercises.title: Basic Inheritance: Vehicle and Car
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Create a class hierarchy where `Car` publicly inherits from `Vehicle`. Demonstrate that the derived class inherits the base class's data and behavior.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `brand` (no spaces, 1-50 characters).
- Line 2: an integer `year`.
- Line 3: an integer `doors`.

## Output

Exactly two lines:
1. `Vehicle: <brand> (<year>)`
2. `Car: <brand> (<year>), <doors> doors`

## Requirements

- Define a class `Vehicle` with public members `std::string brand{}` and `int year{}`.
- `Vehicle` has a public `void printInfo() const` that prints `Vehicle: <brand> (<year>)`.
- Define a class `Car` that publicly inherits from `Vehicle`.
- `Car` adds a public member `int doors{}`.
- `Car` has a public `void printCarInfo() const` that prints `Car: <brand> (<year>), <doors> doors`. Access `brand` and `year` directly (they are inherited).
- In `main`, read `brand`, `year`, and `doors` from stdin. Create a `Car`, assign all three fields, then call `printInfo()` followed by `printCarInfo()`.

exercises.title: Inheritance hierarchy: Person, Student, GradStudent
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a three-level inheritance chain: `GradStudent` inherits from `Student`, which inherits from `Person`. Each level adds one piece of data. Print a summary that uses fields from all three levels, demonstrating that inheritance is transitive.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `name` (no spaces, 1-50 characters).
- Line 2: an integer `age`.
- Line 3: an integer `studentId`.
- Line 4: a string `thesis` (no spaces, 1-100 characters, use underscores for spaces).

## Output

Exactly three lines:
1. `Person: <name>, age <age>`
2. `Student: <name>, id <studentId>`
3. `GradStudent: <name>, thesis: <thesis>`

## Requirements

- Define class `Person` with public members `std::string name{}` and `int age{}`. Add a public `void printPerson() const` that prints `Person: <name>, age <age>`.
- Define class `Student` that publicly inherits from `Person`. Add a public member `int studentId{}`. Add a public `void printStudent() const` that prints `Student: <name>, id <studentId>`.
- Define class `GradStudent` that publicly inherits from `Student`. Add a public member `std::string thesis{}`. Add a public `void printGrad() const` that prints `GradStudent: <name>, thesis: <thesis>`.
- In `main`, read all four inputs, create a `GradStudent`, assign all fields, and call `printPerson()`, `printStudent()`, and `printGrad()` in that order.
- Do not use constructors with parameters -- assign each field individually after default construction.

test_cases.label: Alice, age 25, id 1001, thesis on compilers
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Person: Alice, age 25
Student: Alice, id 1001
GradStudent: Alice, thesis: Compiler_Optimization

test_cases.label: Bob, age 30, id 2050, thesis on robotics
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Person: Bob, age 30
Student: Bob, id 2050
GradStudent: Bob, thesis: Robotics_Navigation

test_cases.label: Zara, age 22, id 9999, thesis on networks
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Person: Zara, age 22
Student: Zara, id 9999
GradStudent: Zara, thesis: Network_Security
